answer:
In the Urdu corpus—primarily poetic works by Allama Iqbal (*Bang-e-Dara* Parts 1–3), Mirza Ghalib (*Diwan-e-Ghalib*), and Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq (*Diwan-e-Zauq*)—"shauq" (شوق, often transliterated as *shauq*, *shauk*, or *zauq*) appears frequently to denote a sensory or natural *longing*, *inclination*, *taste*, or *yearning* for something, such as the desire to see (*shauq-e-deed* or *zauq-e-deed*), travel, or experience.[^1][^2][^3] It conveys enthusiasm or appetite, often tied to worldly or immediate pursuits, without implying total self-annihilation.[^4]

"Ishq" (عشق), by contrast, signifies a deeper, more intense and transformative *passion* or *love*—often anguished, existential, or divine—that overwhelms the self, as in *Dard-e-Ishq* ("The Anguish of Love").[^5] It appears in contexts of ultimate devotion, where mere visual or sensory desire falls short.[^6]

A key distinction emerges in Iqbal's *Koshish-e-Natamam* (*Perpetual Striving*), using natural metaphors for graded desire: streams (*soton*) feel *shauq* toward rivers (a basic, flowing inclination), while the sea (*behr*) feels *ishq* toward rivers (a profounder pull).[^7] Similarly, in another poem, superficial *shauq* for seeing (*deed ka shauq*) is urged to yield to the inward vision of *ishq*, as claiming love without closing the eyes is mere pretense.[^8]

The corpus offers no explicit prose definitions (it is poetic), but usage portrays *shauq* as preparatory or lesser yearning versus *ishq*'s all-consuming fire.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 3 ("Gulzar-e-hast-o-bud na begana-waar dekh"), [p-?](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/03-gulzar-e-hast-o-bud-na-begana-waar-dekh/transliteration/#p-?).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 10 ("Na aate, humein is mein takraar kya thi"), [p-?](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/10-na-aate-humein-is-mein-takraar-kya-thi/transliteration/#p-?).

[^3]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, Ch. 6 ("Aamad-e-khat se hua hai sard jo bazaar-e-dost"), [p-88df93](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/06-aamad-e-khat-se-hua-hai-sard-jo-bazaar-e-dost/transliteration/#p-88df93).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 13 ("Zahir ki aankh se na tamasha kare koi"), [p-464c98](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/13-zahir-ki-aankh-se-na-tamasha-kare-koi/transliteration/#p-464c98).

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 17 ("Dard-e-Ishq"), full chapter, [translation](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/).

[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 12 ("Tere ishq ki inteha chahta hoon"), [p-df4797](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/12-tere-ishq-ki-inteha-chahta-hoon/transliteration/#p-df4797).

[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, Ch. 17 ("Koshish-e-Natamam"), [p-95469e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-95469e) (transl. "The springs for rivers yearn..."); cf. transliteration [full chapter](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/transliteration/).

[^8]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 13 ("Zahir ki aankh se na tamasha kare koi"), [p-464c98](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/13-zahir-ki-aankh-se-na-tamasha-kare-koi/transliteration/#p-464c98).
